clean_story: Tiny energy packets.  Light’s hidden rules. "Max Planck" refers to the physicist who discovered that energy isn’t smooth—it comes in fixed chunks, like stairs instead of a ramp. In 1900, scientists struggled to explain why hot objects glow certain colors. Planck’s math showed energy is released in precise bundles—quanta—each sized by a number now called Planck’s constant. Imagine a fountain spraying single drops, not a steady stream. —That’s how light and heat behave. His equation (E = hν) linked energy (E) to frequency (ν), with h as the tiny, unchanging step between levels. This solved the "ultraviolet catastrophe" (where old theories predicted infinite energy) and birthed quantum physics. Planck’s constant (6. 626 × 10⁻³⁴ joule-seconds) is so small, we don’t notice it daily—like pixels invisible to the naked eye. Yet it governs lasers, LEDs, and even how stars shine. Three related subjects are energy fundamentals, quantum leaps, and blackbody radiation.
